title: Add and subtract fractions word problems
description: Practice solving fraction addition and subtraction word problems. The fractions in these problems have unlike denominators.
topic_path: Adding and subtracting fractions with unlike denominators word problems
Add and subtract fractions word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
The partial table below shows the fraction of fish in Kathy's tank of different types.

Type of fish | Fraction of total
- | - | -
Tetras | $\dfrac16$
Guppies | $\dfrac25$
Goldfish | $\dfrac14$
$\dots$ | $\dots$

**What fraction of Kathy's fish are either tetras or guppies?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] of the fish
hints:
We can add to find the fraction of Kathy's fish that are either tetras or guppies. 


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}}+\blueE{\dfrac{2}{5}}=\text{total tetras \& guppies}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD6$ and $\blueD{5}$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD6$ and $\blueD{5}$ is $\greenD{30}$.

$\dfrac{\maroonD{1}\times 5}{\maroonD{6}\times 5} = \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{30}}$  

$\dfrac{\blueD{2}\times 6}{\blueD{5}\times 6} = \blueD{\dfrac{12}{30}}$

Now, we can add our fractions.

$\begin{align}\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{30}} + \blueD{\dfrac{12}{30}} &= \dfrac{\maroonD{5} + \blueD{12}}{30} \\\\
&= \dfrac{17}{30}\end{align}$
$\dfrac{17}{30}$ of Kathy's fish are tetras or guppies.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jerry played with $3$ friends today, all at different times.  He played with Tommy for $1\dfrac{7}{8}$ hours, then Nathan for $1\dfrac{3}{4}$ hours, then Chris for $\dfrac{3}{2}$ hours.

**How many hours did Jerry play with his $3$ friends today?** 
 
[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  hours
hints:
To find the total number of hours that Jerry played with his friends, we need to add.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{1\dfrac78} + \blueD{1\dfrac34} + \purpleD{\dfrac32} = \text{ hours spent playing}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD8$, $\blueD{4}$, and $\purpleD{2}$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD8$, $\blueD{4}$, and $\purpleD{2}$ is $\greenD{8}$.

$\dfrac{\blueD{3}\times 2}{\blueD{4}\times 2} = \blueD{\dfrac{6}{8}}$

$\dfrac{\purpleD{3}\times 4}{\purpleD{2}\times 4} = \purpleD{\dfrac{12}{8}}$

Now we can add our fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\maroonD{1} &\maroonD{\dfrac7{8}}\\\\
&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{6}{8}}\\
+&\purpleD{0}&\purpleD{\dfrac{12}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{25}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

We can replace $ \greenD{\dfrac{25}{8}}$ with $\greenD3 \greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\greenD3\\
&\maroonD{1} &\maroonD{\dfrac7{8}}\\\\
&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{6}{8}}\\
+&\purpleD{0}&\purpleD{\dfrac{12}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

Now we can add our whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD3\\
&\maroonD{1} &\maroonD{\dfrac7{8}}\\\\
&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{6}{8}}\\
+&\purpleD{0}&\purpleD{\dfrac{12}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&5&{\dfrac{1}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

Jerry played with his three friends for $\dfrac{41}{8}$ hours today.

This can also be written as $5\dfrac{1}{8}$ hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
The first team member in a $3$-person relay race must run $2 \dfrac{1}{4}$ laps, the second team member must run $1 \dfrac{1}{2}$ laps, and the third team member must run $3 \dfrac{5}{8}$ laps.

**How many laps in all must each team run?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] laps
hints:
To find the total number of laps, we can add.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{2 \dfrac{1}{4}} + \blueD{1 \dfrac{1}{2}} + \purpleD{3 \dfrac{5}{8}} = \text{ total number of laps}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD4$, $\blueD{2}$, and $\purpleD{8}$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD4$, $\blueD{2}$, and $\purpleD{8}$ is $\greenD{8}$.

$\dfrac{\maroonD{1}\times 2}{\maroonD{4}\times 2} = \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times 4}{\blueD{2}\times 4} = \blueD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$

Now we can add our fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\maroonD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac2{8}}\\\\
&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\
+&\purpleD{3}&\purpleD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{11}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

We can replace $ \greenD{\dfrac{11}{8}}$ with $\greenD1 \greenD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$.  

$\begin{align}
&\greenD1\\
&\maroonD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac2{8}}\\\\
&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\
+&\purpleD{3}&\purpleD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\greenD{\dfrac{3}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

Now we can add our whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\greenD1\\
&\maroonD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac2{8}}\\\\
&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{4}{8}}\\
+&\purpleD{3}&\purpleD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&7&{\dfrac{3}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

Each team will run ${7 \dfrac{3}{8}}$ laps.

This can also be written as ${\dfrac{59}{8}}$ laps.

assessment_items.question_content:
Johnny and Elizabeth were playing a video game and trying to get all of the treasure boxes.  Johnny got $2\dfrac{1}{3}$ treasure boxes.  Elizabeth got $1\dfrac{5}{9}$ treasure boxes.  

**Together, Johnny and Elizabeth got how many treasure boxes?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] treasure boxes
hints:
To find the total amount of treasure boxes that Johnny and Elizabeth got together, we need to add.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{2\dfrac{1}{3}} + \blueD{1\dfrac{5}{9}} = \text{ total amount of treasure boxes}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD3$ and $\blueD{9}$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD3$ and $\blueD{9}$ is $\greenD{9}$.

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times 3}{\blueD{3}\times 3} = \blueD{\dfrac{3}{9}}$

Now we can add our fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\maroonD{2} &\maroonD{\dfrac3{9}}\\\\
+&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{5}{9}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\greenD3&\greenD{\dfrac{8}{9}}\\
\end{align}$
Johnny and Elizabeth's combined score is $3\dfrac{8}{9}$ treasure boxes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mrs. Alvarez asked her class how many hours of TV they watched per day (to the nearest half hour).  The results are shown below.

Hours of TV | Fraction of class
-|:-:
$0$ to $1$ | $\dfrac{1}{6}$
$1 \large\frac{1}{2}$ to $2$ | $\dfrac{5}{12}$
$2 \large\frac{1}{2}$ to $3$ | $\dfrac{1}{4}$
More than $3$ | $\dfrac{1}{6}$

**What fraction of the students watch between $0$ and $2$ hours of TV a day?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of the students
hints:
To find the total fraction of students who watch $0$ to $2$ hours of TV, we can add.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{6}} + \blueD{\dfrac{5}{12}} = \text{ total fraction of students}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD6$ and $\blueD{12}$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD6$ and $\blueD{12}$ is $\greenD{12}$.

$\dfrac{\maroonD{1}\times 2}{\maroonD{6}\times 2} = \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{12}}$

Now, we can add our fractions.

$\begin{align}\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{12}} + \blueD{\dfrac{5}{12}} &= \dfrac{\maroonD{2} + \blueD{5}}{12}\\\\
&= \dfrac{7}{12}\end{align}$
The fraction of the students who watch between $0$ and $2$ hours of TV a day is $\dfrac{7}{12}$ .

assessment_items.question_content:
Ahoy there, matey! Our pirate ship had $\dfrac{5}{6}$ of a treasure chest full of gold.  Then we plundered an enemy ship and took their $\dfrac{3}{8}$ of a treasure chest full of gold.

**How many treasure chests full of gold do we have now?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] treasure chests
hints:
To find the total number of treasure chests that we have now, we need to add.

[[☃ image 1]]



$\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{6}} + \blueD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD6$ and $\blueD{8}$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD6$ and $\blueD{8}$ is $\greenD{24}$.

$\dfrac{\maroonD{5}\times 4}{\maroonD{6}\times 4} = \maroonD{\dfrac{20}{24}}$

$\dfrac{\blueD{3}\times 3}{\blueD{8}\times 3} = \blueD{\dfrac{9}{24}}$

Now, we can add our fractions.

$\begin{align}\maroonD{\dfrac{20}{24}} + \blueD{\dfrac{9}{24}} &= \dfrac{\maroonD{20} + \blueD{9}}{24}\\\\
&= \dfrac{29}{24}\end{align}$

We now have $\dfrac{29}{24}$ treasure chests full of gold.

This answer can also be written as $1 \dfrac{5}{24}$ treasure chests full of gold.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jimmy is trying to dive down and touch the bottom of the pool.  The table below shows how far down he made it on each dive.

Dive attempt | Fraction of the distance to the pool bottom
- | - | -
Dive $1$ | $\dfrac13$
Dive $2$ | $\dfrac35$
Dive $3$ | $\dfrac56$

**Jimmy's second dive was deeper than his first dive by what fraction of the pool?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of the pool
hints:
To find the difference in the depths, we need to subtract.  



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{\dfrac{3}{5}} - \blueD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subtract 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\purpleD{5}$ and $\blueD{3}$?

The least common multiple of $\purpleD5$ and $\blueD{3}$ is $\greenD{15}$.

$\dfrac{\purpleD{3}\times3}{\purpleD{5}\times3} = \purpleD{\dfrac{9}{15}}$

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times5}{\blueD{3}\times5} = \blueD{\dfrac{5}{15}}$

Now, we can subtract our fractions.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{9}{15}} - \blueD{\dfrac{5}{15}} = \dfrac{\purpleD{9} - \blueD{5}}{15} = \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{15}}$
Jimmy's second dive was deeper by $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{15}}$ of the pool.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amir is sorting his stamp collection.  He made a chart of the fraction of stamps from each country in his collection.  

$\dfrac{7}{12}$ of Amir's stamps are from either Morocco or Spain.   

Country | Fraction of stamps
-|:-:
France | $\dfrac{1}{3}$
Spain | $?$
Morocco | $\dfrac16$
Slovenia |  $\dfrac{1}{12}$

**What fraction of Amir's stamps are from Spain?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of Amir's stamps
hints:
To find the total fraction of the stamps that are from Spain, we need to subtract.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{\dfrac{7}{12}} - \blueD{\dfrac{1}{6}} = \maroonD{\text{ Stamps from Spain}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subtract 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\purpleD{12}$ and $\blueD{6}$?

The least common multiple of $\purpleD{12}$ and $\blueD{6}$ is $\greenD{12}$.

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times2}{\blueD{6}\times2} = \blueD{\dfrac{2}{12}}$

Now, we can subtract our fractions.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{7}{12}} - \blueD{\dfrac{2}{12}} = \dfrac{\purpleD{7} - \blueD{2}}{12} = \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{12}}$
$\dfrac{5}{12}$ of Amir's stamp collection comes from Spain.

assessment_items.question_content:
Farmer George is painting $3$ chicken coops.    He started painting this morning.  Now he only has $1\dfrac34$ of a chicken coop left to paint this afternoon.

**How many chicken coops did Farmer George paint this morning?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] chicken coops
hints:
To see how many chicken coops Farmer George painted this morning, we need to subtract.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{3} - \blueD{1\dfrac{3}{4}} = \maroonD{\text{ chicken coops painted}}$

Let's subtract our fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\purpleD{3} &\purpleD{\dfrac04}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We cannot subtract because $\purpleD{\dfrac04}$ is **less** than $\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

Now, let's subtract the fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\purpleD{2}}{\cancel{\purpleD{3}}} &\purpleD{\dfrac{\overset{4}{\cancel{0}}}{4}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac14}
\end{align}$

Next, let's subtract the whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\purpleD{2}}{\cancel{\purpleD{3}}} &\purpleD{\dfrac{\overset{4}{\cancel{0}}}{4}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\\
\hline{}\\\\
&\maroonD1&\maroonD{\dfrac14}
\end{align}$

Farmer George painted $1\dfrac{1}{4}$ chicken coops this morning.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{5}{4}$ .

assessment_items.question_content:
Two lions, Fluffy and Fireball, met at the zoo.  Fluffy's tail is $1\dfrac{1}{3}$ of a meter long.  Fireball's tail is $1\dfrac{1}{4}$ of a meter long.  

**How much longer is Fluffy's tail than Fireball's tail?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a meter longer
hints:
To find the difference in the tail lengths, we need to subtract.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{1\dfrac{1}{3}} - \blueD{1\dfrac{1}{4}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subtract 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\purpleD{3}$ and $\blueD{4}$?

The least common multiple of $\purpleD3$ and $\blueD{4}$ is $\greenD{12}$.

$\dfrac{\purpleD{1}\times4}{\purpleD{3}\times4} = \purpleD{\dfrac{4}{12}}$

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times3}{\blueD{4}\times3} = \blueD{\dfrac{3}{12}}$

Let's subtract our fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\purpleD{1} &\purpleD{\dfrac4{12}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{3}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac14}
\end{align}$

Next, let's subtract our whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\purpleD{1} &\purpleD{\dfrac4{12}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{3}{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\maroonD0&\maroonD{\dfrac1{12}}
\end{align}$
Fluffy's tail is $\dfrac{1}{12}$ of a meter longer than Fireball's tail.

assessment_items.question_content:
Harry's spell casting class lasts for $2\dfrac{5}{6}$ hours each day.  Today, he has been in the class for $1\dfrac{2}{5}$ of an hour so far.

**How much more time is left in the class?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of an hour
hints:
To find the time left in class, we can subtract.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{2\dfrac{5}{6}} - \blueD{1\dfrac{2}{5}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subtract 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\purpleD{6}$ and $\blueD{5}$?

The least common multiple of $\purpleD6$ and $\blueD{5}$ is $\greenD{30}$.

$\dfrac{\purpleD{5}\times5}{\purpleD{6}\times5} = \purpleD{\dfrac{25}{30}}$

$\dfrac{\blueD{2}\times6}{\blueD{5}\times6} = \blueD{\dfrac{12}{30}}$

Now, let's subtract our fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\purpleD{2} &\purpleD{\dfrac{25}{30}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{12}{30}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{13}{30}}
\end{align}$

Last, let's subtract our whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\purpleD{2} &\purpleD{\dfrac{25}{30}}\\\\
-&\blueD{1}&\blueD{\dfrac{12}{30}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\maroonD1&\maroonD{\dfrac{13}{30}}
\end{align}$

There is $1\dfrac{13}{30}$ hours left in class.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{43}{30}$ .

assessment_items.question_content:
Katie and Tyler are working at their lemonade stand.  They have sold $2\dfrac{1}{2}$ pitchers of lemonade and now have $\dfrac13$ of a pitcher left to sell.

**How many pitchers of lemonade did they start with?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a pitcher
hints:
Katie and Tyler sold $2\dfrac12$ pitchers of lemonade and are left with $\dfrac13$ of a pitcher of lemonade.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{2\dfrac12} + \purpleD{\dfrac13}={\maroonD{?}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\blueD2$ and $\purpleD{3}$?

The least common multiple of $\blueD2$ and $\purpleD{3}$ is $\greenD{6}$.

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times 3}{\blueD{2}\times 3} = \blueD{\dfrac{3}{6}}$

$\dfrac{\purpleD{1}\times 2}{\purpleD{3}\times 2} = \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{6}}$

Now, we can add our fractions.

$\blueD{2\dfrac{3}{6}} + \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{6}} = 2\dfrac{\blueD{3} + \purpleD{2}}{6} =\maroonD{2 \dfrac{5}{6}}$
Katie and Tyler started with $\maroonD{2\dfrac{5}{6}}$ pitchers of lemonade.

assessment_items.question_content:
At the party, Aisha and her friends ate $2 \dfrac{1}{2}$ pizzas.  After the party, there were $1 \dfrac{1}{8}$ pizzas left.  

**How many pizzas were there at the start of the party?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pizzas
hints:
To find the total number of pizzas at the start of the party, we need to add.
[[☃ image 1]]

 $\blueD{2 \dfrac{1}{2}} + \purpleD{1 \dfrac{1}{8}} = \maroonD{\text{ amount of pizza to start}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\blueD2$ and $\purpleD{8}$?

The least common multiple of $\blueD2$ and $\purpleD{8}$ is $\greenD{8}$.

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times 4}{\blueD{2}\times 4} = \blueD{\dfrac{4}{8}}$

Now we can add our fractions.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{2} &\blueD{\dfrac4{8}}\\\\
+&\purpleD{1}&\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

Lastly, we can add our whole numbers.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{2} &\blueD{\dfrac4{8}}\\\\
+&\purpleD{1}&\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\maroonD3&\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}\\
\end{align}$

There were $\maroonD{3\dfrac{5}{8}}$ pizzas at the start of the party.

This can also be written as $\maroonD{\dfrac{29}{8}}$ pizzas.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jennifer used $\dfrac{2}{7}$ of a meter of ribbon to make bows for her cousins.  Now, she has $\dfrac{10}{21}$ of a meter of ribbon left.  

**How much ribbon did Jennifer start with?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a meter
hints:
To find the amount of ribbon Jennifer started with, we can add.

[[☃ image 1]]



$\blueD{\dfrac{2}{7}} + \purpleD{\dfrac{10}{21}} = \maroonD{\text{ amount of ribbon to start}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\blueD7$ and $\purpleD{21}$?

The least common multiple of $\blueD7$ and $\purpleD{21}$ is $\greenD{21}$.

$\dfrac{\blueD{2}\times 3}{\blueD{7}\times 3} = \blueD{\dfrac{6}{21}}$

Now, we can add our fractions.

$\blueD{\dfrac{6}{21}} + \purpleD{\dfrac{10}{21}} = \dfrac{\blueD{6} + \purpleD{10}}{21} =\maroonD{\dfrac{16}{21}}$
Jennifer started with $\dfrac{16}{21}$ of a meter of ribbon.

assessment_items.question_content:
Heba ate $\dfrac{1}{12}$ of a box of cereal.  Now the box is $\dfrac{3}{4}$ full. 

**What fraction of a full box was there before Heba ate?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a full box
hints:
Heba ate $\blueD{\dfrac{1}{12}}$ of a box of cereal and there is $\purpleD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ of a box left. 

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{12}}+\purpleD{\dfrac{3}{4}} = \maroonD{\text{ amount of cereal to start}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can add. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\blueD{12}$ and $\purpleD{4}$?

The least common multiple of $\blueD{12}$ and $\purpleD{4}$ is $\greenD{12}$.

$\dfrac{\purpleD{3}\times 3}{\purpleD{4}\times 3} = \purpleD{\dfrac{9}{12}}$

Now, we can add our fractions.

$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{12}} + \purpleD{\dfrac{9}{12}} = \dfrac{\blueD{1} + \purpleD{9}}{12} =\maroonD{\dfrac{10}{12}}$

There was  $\dfrac{10}{12}$ of a box of cereal before Heba ate.

This answer can also be written as $\dfrac{5}{6}$ of a box.

assessment_items.question_content:
Silvia is growing trees and studying their heights. Her data is shown below.

Tree type | Height (meters)
-|:-:
Birch | $3 \dfrac{1}{4}$
Spruce | $2 \dfrac{7}{8}$
Oak | $3 \dfrac{1}{2}$

**What is the difference between the heights of the birch and spruce trees?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meter
hints:
The Birch tree is  $3\dfrac14$ meters tall and the Spruce tree is $2\dfrac78$ meters tall.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{3\dfrac{1}{4}} - \blueD{2\dfrac{7}{8}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subtract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\purpleD4$ and $\blueD8$?

The least common multiple of $\purpleD4$ and $\blueD8$ is $\greenD{8}$.

$\purpleD{\dfrac14}=\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{4}} \times {\dfrac{2}{2}} = \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$

Now, let's subtract.

$\begin{align}
&\purpleD{3} &\purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
-&\blueD{2}&\blueD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We need to regroup $\purpleD{3}\purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\blueD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{3} \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}} &=\purpleD{2 + 1} \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\purpleD{2} + \purpleD{\dfrac88} + \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\purpleD{2} + \purpleD{\dfrac{10}{8}}
\end{align}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator and our first fraction is **greater** than our second fraction, we can subtract.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\purpleD{2}}{\cancel{\purpleD{3}} }&\purpleD{\dfrac{\overset{\purpleD{10}}{\cancel{2}}}{8}}\\\\
-&\blueD{2}&\blueD{\dfrac{7}{8}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\maroonD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}
\end{align}$
The difference in height between the Birch and Spruce trees is $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$ of a meter.

assessment_items.question_content:
Heidi grew her hair out for many years. Her hair was $1\dfrac{1}{3}$ of a meter long.  She donated her hair by cutting off $\dfrac{5}{6}$ of a meter.

**How long is Heidi's hair now?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a meter long
hints:
To find the length of Heidi's hair now, we need to subtract.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{1\dfrac{1}{3}} - \blueD{\dfrac{5}{6}} = \purpleD{\text{ length of hair now}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subtract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD3$ and $\blueD6$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD3$ and $\blueD6$ is $\greenD{6}$.

$\maroonD{\dfrac13}=\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{3}} \times {\dfrac{2}{2}} = \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}$

Now, let's subtract.

$\begin{align}
&\maroonD{1} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\blueD{\dfrac{5}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
\end{align}$

We need to regroup $\maroonD{1}\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\blueD{\dfrac{5}{6}}$.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{1} \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}} &=\maroonD{\dfrac66} + \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{6}}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{6}}
\end{align}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator and our first fraction is **greater** than our second fraction, we can subtract.

$\begin{align}
&\overset{\maroonD{0}}{\cancel{\maroonD{1}} }&\maroonD{\dfrac{\overset{\maroonD{8}}{\cancel{2}}}{6}}\\\\
-&\blueD{0}&\blueD{\dfrac{5}{6}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\maroonD{0}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{6}}
\end{align}$

The length of Heidi's hair now is $\dfrac{3}{6}$ of a meter.

This answer can also be written as $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of a meter.

assessment_items.question_content:
The results of an election are shown in the table below.

Candidate | Fraction of votes
-|:-:
Johnson | $\dfrac{1}{9}$
Smith | $\dfrac{7}{18}$
Dragovic | $\dfrac{1}{2}$

**What is the difference between the fractions of votes received by Dragovic and Johnson?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
To find the difference in the fractions of votes, we need to subtract.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}} - \blueD{\dfrac{1}{9}} = \purpleD{\text{ difference}}$

Our denominators need to be the same so we can subtract. 

What is the least common multiple for the denominators $\maroonD2$ and $\blueD9$?

The least common multiple of $\maroonD2$ and $\blueD9$ is $\greenD{18}$

$\dfrac{\maroonD{1}\times9}{\maroonD{2}\times9} = \maroonD{\dfrac{9}{18}}$

$\dfrac{\blueD{1}\times2}{\blueD{9}\times2} = \blueD{\dfrac{2}{18}}$
The difference in votes between Dragovic and Johnson is $\dfrac{7}{18}$ .
